State of California

Memorandum

To    : File                                          Date  : October 3, 1988


From  : Fair Political Practices Commission
         John G. McLean

Subject: Advice Request No. T-88-365

         Spoke today with Mary Hansen.  Advised her that under emergency
Regulation 18901, the Mayor's column may not be included in a mass
mailing.  Further advised her that the newsletter may not contain
the name, signature, photograph, or any other reference to an
elected officer.

JGM:ld:T88365

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 21, 1988


Mary Getty Hansen
Town Clerk
142 Bolinas Road
Fairfax, CA  94930

Re:  88-365

Dear Ms. Hansen:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on September 20, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice.  If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed.  If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Diane M. Griffiths for
General Counsel

DMG:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

TOWN OF FAIRFAX
142 BOLINAS ROAD, FAIRFAX, CALIFORNIA 94930 (415) 453-1584

MAYOR
Wendy Baker

TOWN ADMINISTRATOR
Charles K. Cate

COUNCIL
David Clark
Frank Egger
Ken Lippi
Carol Sherman

September 13,1988

Alice Hughes
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 870
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Alice:

Recently we received the attached memorandum from the League of California Cities outlining the amendment to government Code Section 89001 by Proposition 73, which reads in part: "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense."

The above has always been considered by me to be in connection with the campaigning for an election by candidates and in connection with a newsletter published for the citizens of a city and sent to them. My question to you is can we still publish the city newsletter (we have enclosed two) and can the Message from the Mayor still appear?

The Council is now considering publishing its Newsletter for this year and we need to be informed as to the correctness of printing the Message from the Mayor and the newsletter itself.

We hope you enjoy reading them and thank you in advance for your comments on the above.

Cordially,

Mary Getty Hansen, CMC
Town Clerk

enclosures

League of California Cities
1400 K STREET • SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • (916) 444-5790

California Cities
Work Together

AUGUST 1988

TO: All City Attorneys

FROM: Paul Valle-Riestra, Staff Attorney

RE: Newsletters and Other Mass Mailings

Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Government Code to
read, "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense."

Recently we sent to you an advice letter from the Fair Political
Practices Commission (FPPC) dated July 1, concerning mass
mailings.

On July 26, the FPPC adopted an emergency regulation on mass
mailings, a copy of which is attached. The emergency regulation
appears to follow the advice letter. Note that the advice letter
at page 5 states that a newsletter may contain a standard
roster-type listing of elected officials. While the emergency
regulation does not specifically authorize this listing, FPPC
staff has told us that these listings are still permitted.
However, you may wish to request an advice letter on this subject
before including such a listing in a newsletter.

One of the Commissioners questioned whether they have the legal
authority to permit certain mass mailings when the text of
Proposition 73 appears to prohibit publicly funded mass mailings
across the board. FPPC staff gave the preliminary opinion that
they do have this authority. However, the FPPC directed staff to
prepare a legal analysis of this authority for the September
meeting. At that meeting, the FPPC is expected to pre-notice a
permanent regulation on mass mailings for consideration at the
November meeting.

massmail.exec

TOWN OF FAIRFAX
142 BOLINAS ROAD, FAIRFAX, CALIFORNIA 94930 (415) 453-1584

MAYOR
Wendy Baker

COUNCIL
David Clark

Carol Sherman

TOWN ADMINISTRATOR
Charles K. Cote

September 15,1988

Alice Hughes
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 870
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Alice:

Recently we received the attached memorandum from the League
of California Cities outlining the amendment to Government
Code Section 89001 by Proposition 73, which reads in part:
"No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense."

The above has always been considered by me to be in connection
with the campaigning for an election by candidates and not in
connection with a newsletter published for the citizens of a
city and sent to them. My question to you is can we still publish
the city newsletter (we have enclosed two) and can the Message
from the Mayor still appear?

The Council is now considering publishing its Newsletter for this
year and we need to be informed as to the correctness of printing
the Message from the Mayor and the newsletter itself.

We hope you enjoy reading them and thank you in advance for your
comments on the above.

Cordially,

Mary Getti Hansen, CMC
Town Clerk

enclosures

Alternative 1

Amend 2 Cal Code of Regs. Sect. 18901 to read:

18901. Prohibition on Newsletter or Mass Mailing

-(a) The prohibition contained in Government Code
Section 89001 against any legislative newsletter or other mass
mailing sent at public expense by or on behalf of any elected
state officer after the elected state officer has filed a
declaration of candidacy for any office terminates when:
-(1) The elections for which the declaration of
candidacy was filed have been conducted or the
candidate has been defeated; or
-(2) The officer obtains a court order revoking
his declaration of candidacy.

-(b)(a) A mailing is a mass mailing if 200 or more
identical or nearly identical substantially similar pieces of
mail are sent in a calendar month, provided, however, that mass
mailings do not include mailings which contain only information
or material sent in response to specific requests contained in
written correspondence, petitions, oral inquiries or coupons
and the requests were not solicited by the elected officer, or
by any third person at the behest of the elected officer.

-(c)(b) A mailing will not be deemed to be sent "by or
on behalf of any elected state officer" prohibited by Section
89001 if the mailing meets all of the following criteria:

7/18/88                                1                    18901A

(4) Mailings to persons subject to a government

program administered by a governmental officer when

such mailings are essential to the functioning of the

program; or

(5) Mailings required by statute, ordinance or

court order.

AUTHORITY: Gov. Code Section 83112
REFERENCE: Gov. Code Sections 82041.5, 89001

7/18/88                    3                    18901A

FAIRFAX TOWN
NEWSLETTER

PUBLISHED BY THE TOWN OF FAIRFAX                                    NOVEMBER · DECEMBER, 1987

comments about the services we provide. Let us know how we're  tions. Write or call Town Hall, and remember that the Fairfax
doing.                                                         Parks and Recreation Commission meets the fourth Thursday

[Signature: Glou Duncan]

         FRONT                                          SIDE


Bandstand Proposed For Bolinas Road Park

A group of private citizens have proposed a unique addition to the Bolinas Road park where the Fairfax Festival is held each
year. Plans are in the works to create a non-profit organization to raise money for construction of an old fashion bandstand. The
town budgeted $2,000 as seed money for this effort. This will be a fun project which will benefit the entire community. The goal
is to have the bandstand in place for a summer concert series in 1988. For details call Robert Kennedy, 457-3847.

Peri Park—
Many hours
were spent
installing the new
equipment!

Improvements for Peri Park

Using State Bond Act monies, Peri Park has received a $24,000
face lift. New equipment, sand, benches, trash containers and
a walkway are in place. The improvements have proven to be
a big hit with the Fairfax younger set and their parents. The
Friends of Peri Park, a volunteer group of parents and concerned
citizens, spearheaded the drive to upgrade the park area, and
spent many hours installing the equipment. The entire Town can
be proud of the final product.

New Look For Sir Francis Drake Boulevard
And Other Improvement Projects

Undergrounding utility lines and the planing of twenty-two
new street trees along Sir Francis Drake Boulevard will result
in an open and more attractive vista in the area from 2000 Sir
Francis Drake west to Marin Road. The majority of work is completed and the trees will be planted in January once all the utility lines are removed. The trees were paid for with Town funds,
but the cost of the $350,000 Undergrounding Project was almost
entirely funded by PG&E.

Also soon to be installed will be four hundred feet of new safety guardrail on Sir Francis Drake Boulevard on either side of
Marin Road. The estimated cost is $44,000, but because of a
State Grant, the Town's share will be only 10% of that cost.

Also slated for the summer of 1988 will be completion of the
repaving of Marinda Drive, at an estimated cost of $44,000.

Another project planned will be funded jointly by the Town
and the Golden Gate Transit District. It includes construction
of a sidewalk between the stairs and the handicapped ramp. This
will provide greater safety and convenience for commuters getting off the bus in the evening.

Typesetting, Graphic Camera Work & Production — Van Norman/Associates

P. & D
Fairfax, CA
Permit #43

Postal Customer
Fairfax, California 94930

Watch For Santa . . . in the downtown area on December
13th and 20th in the morning hours. He and his elfs will also
be found at the lawn area outside Town Hall from 12 P.M.
to 3 P.M. these same days.

Call Santa on December 21, 22 and 23, from 6 to 8 P.M.
at 453-1587

FAIRFAX TOWN
NEWSLETTER

PUBLISHED BY THE TOWN OF FAIRFAX                                           DECEMBER, 1986

Message From The Mayor                          Your Town and Its Government

Welcome to the first issue of the Fairfax Newsletter! Because
the Council believes that communication with all of our residents
is a priority we have undertaken this little venture, knowing full
well that the Newsletter will never pose a threat to the established
press of Marin County. However, there are interesting sidelights
about your town government we would like to share with you,
as well as being able to keep you informed of our goals and
policies as they are developed.
We would like to hear from you; are there issues you would
like more fully explained; objectives that need to be more clearly
defined, and most of all, do you like the Newsletter?

[signature]

Will You Volunteer for Fairfax?

The strength of a local community builds on the willingness
of the people who live in it to help take care of each other, watching out for children and teenagers, keeping parks safe and clean,
taking care of shut-ins, having fun together like the Fairfax
Festival and block parties. Volunteering is one way we care
of each other. However we often do not know who needs help
or what needs to be done. Some kind of local clearing-house
such as the San Anselmo Volunteer Effort funded by the town
could help us in Fairfax.

There are some people in Fairfax who would like to get
together in the New Year, Wednesday, January 14, at 7:30 P.M.,
at the Fairfax Women's Club, to discuss the needs of Fairfax
and to see if we can get a Fairfax Volunteer Program up and
going. Part of the agenda will be devoted to discussing if having
a Town Meeting in 1987 can happen. People who are interested
should contact Michael Maeder at 457-5723.

Fairfax has a population of approximately 7,400 in a 2.2 square
mile area, with 27 miles of roads. We have three schools and
four churches.

Located at 142 Bolinas Road, along with the Police Department, the Town Hall is open from 8:30 A.M. to 5 P.M. Monday through Thursday, and 8:30 A.M. to noon on Fridays. Our
phone number is 453-1584. The Police Department is open 24
hours a day. The Fire Department, run by the Ross Valley Fire
Service, is around the corner on Park Road.

The Town has twenty-nine employees: seventeen in the police
department, six in streets and park maintenance, and six in
administration, finance, building and planning.

There is a five member Town Council who are elected to office for four year terms. The Council appoints a Town Administrator to carry out its policies, goals and objectives.

Councilmembers include Mayor Carol Sherman, Vice-Mayor
Gloria Duncan, Frank Egger, Ken Lippi and Wendy Baker.

Key staff members are:
Charles R. Cate, Town Administrator
Walt Potter, Chief of Police
Bill Street, Superintendent of Streets
Nancy M. Marion, Director of Community Development
Mary Getty Hansen, Town Clerk

We welcome your comments on how the Town can improve
its public service. Do not hesitate to call us.

ON BEHALF OF THE TOWN COUNCIL AND STAFF
WE WOULD LIKE TO WISH YOU ALL A VERY HAPPY
AND HEALTHY HOLIDAY SEASON.

The old Fairfax 1929 Seagrave Fire Engine recently restored thanks to the Fire Warden's Club.

Update on 1986 Storm Damage Construction Projects
The Town was hit hard last February with road and hillside slipout problems that required major repair work. Construction projects to repair damage have been completed on upper Scenic across from 184, lower Scenic across from 355, and at the end of Pine Drive. One other major repair remains at 223 Scenic and that contract is currently under way. We appreciate the patience of the residents of Manor Hill during the construction period.

Fixing last year's damage on Scenic Road.

Parking Enforcement Program Under Way
Under the direction of Chief Walt Potter, the Fairfax Police Department is taking a fair, but consistent, approach to enforcement of the Town's parking regulations. There are two code sections which can be particularly hazardous if not enforced, especially in the hillside areas of Town. You can help by:
• Curbing, blocking or turning wheels into a bank, and
• Parking to leave a minimum of 12' clearance on the roadway.

BULK RATE
U.S. Postage
PAID
Fairfax, CA
Permit #43

Building Permits and Public Safety
The Town has a full service building department which is committed to assisting the public in the processing and approval of construction related permits and inspecting work in progress. The construction codes are intended to safeguard the public against unsafe building practices and materials. The majority of construction, including reconstruction and repairs, require securing a building permit from the Town. If you have doubts as to whether a building permit is required, please contact the Building/Planning Department at 453-1584 during Town Hall business hours, prior to commencing any work. We will be most happy to answer your questions and assist you in any way we can.

Postal Customer
Fairfax, California 94930

Happy Holidays!

Typesetting & Production — Van Norman/Associates

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city may publish a newsletter containing a 'Message from the Mayor' without violating Government Code Section 89001's prohibition on mass mailings sent at public expense, particularly regarding inclusion of the Mayor's message and elected officials' references.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under emergency Regulation 18901, the Mayor's column may not be included in a mass mailing. The newsletter may not contain the name, signature, photograph, or any other reference to an elected officer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both formal letter of receipt dated September 21, 1988, and informal memorandum dated October 3, 1988 documenting the actual advice given. The question is reconstructed from the incoming request letter from Mary Hansen (Town Clerk). Document also includes related regulatory materials and newsletter samples. Some pages partially damaged/illegible but key advice clearly stated in McLean memo.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city may publish a newsletter containing a 'Message from the Mayor' without violating Government Code Section 89001's prohibition on mass mailings sent at public expense, particularly regarding inclusion of the Mayor's message and elected officials' references.

CONCLUSION: Under emergency Regulation 18901, the Mayor's column may not be included in a mass mailing. The newsletter may not contain the name, signature, photograph, or any other reference to an elected officer.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry from Town Clerk Mary Hansen regarding whether the Town of Fairfax may publish a city newsletter with a Mayor's message under Proposition 73's ban on mass mailings sent at public expense. FPPC advised that the Mayor's column cannot be included in mass mailings and newsletters cannot reference elected officers.
